eiffel_tower.description: Iron lattice tower built for the 1889 World's Fair; still the most visited paid monument in the city.
eiffel_tower.history: Designed by Gustave Eiffel's team; controversial at first, later embraced as the skyline signature of Paris.
eiffel_tower.significance: Observation decks and night lighting make it both museum piece and living landmark.
facts: Reserve lift tickets online for peak season.
The esplanade is free; climbing the stairs is limited to certain days.

notre_dame_cathedral.description: Medieval Gothic cathedral famous for its west façade, rose windows, and flying buttresses.
notre_dame_cathedral.history: Construction spanned centuries from the 12th century; fire in 2019 led to a major restoration project.
notre_dame_cathedral.significance: Liturgical heart of Paris and setting of Hugo's novel—exterior viewing remains moving.
facts: Queue management changes after reopening; check official notices.
Combine with Sainte-Chapelle nearby on the same island.

arc_de_triomphe.description: Neoclassical triumphal arch at the star junction of twelve avenues; tomb of the Unknown Soldier beneath.
arc_de_triomphe.history: Commissioned by Napoleon; completed in the 1830s; relief sculpture tells revolutionary and imperial stories.
arc_de_triomphe.significance: Terrace views sweep along the axe to La Défense and toward the Louvre.
facts: Use the underground passage—do not cross the roundabout on foot.
July sunsets backlight the Champs-Élysées axis.

sacre_coeur.description: Romano-Byzantine basilica with chalk-white domes atop Montmartre—panoramic over northern Paris.
sacre_coeur.history: Consecrated in 1919; built as national vow after the Franco-Prussian War and Commune.
sacre_coeur.significance: Pilgrimage church and sunset viewpoint above winding village streets.
facts: The dome climb is separate from the nave visit.
Weekend crowds are heavy; early mornings are quieter.

pont_alexandre_iii.description: Beaux-Arts bridge rich with gilt lampposts and stone allegories—favorite film and parade backdrop.
pont_alexandre_iii.history: Opened for the 1900 Exposition Universelle; named for the Russian tsar.
pont_alexandre_iii.significance: Frames Invalides on one side, Grand and Petit Palais on the other.
facts: Sunrise and blue hour emphasize the sculptures.
Expect closures during city events.

centre_pompidou.description: High-tech museum with color-coded ducts and pipes on the façade—modern and contemporary collections inside.
centre_pompidou.history: Renzo Piano and Richard Rogers' competition winner opened in 1977.
centre_pompidou.significance: Major European hub for 20th- and 21st-century art and public library space.
facts: The exterior escalator tube offers city views with a ticket.
The square hosts street performers year-round.

musee_orsay.description: Former railway station housing Impressionist masterpieces, sculpture, and decorative arts.
musee_orsay.history: Gare d'Orsay's train hall became a museum in the 1980s.
musee_orsay.significance: Single visit can cover Monet, Van Gogh, Degas, and the Belle Époque clock views.
facts: Thursday late openings are popular—book ahead.
The riverside façade faces the Louvre across the Seine.

place_de_la_concorde.description: Vast octagonal square with fountains and the Luxor obelisk at the historic heart of royal and revolutionary Paris.
place_de_la_concorde.history: Site of the guillotine during the Terror; obelisk erected in the 1830s.
place_de_la_concorde.significance: Link between the Tuileries, Madeleine, and Champs-Élysées.
facts: Traffic is fast; use marked crossings.
Ferris wheels sometimes appear seasonally.

hotel_des_invalides.description: Baroque military complex with golden dome over Napoleon's tomb and Army Museum collections.
hotel_des_invalides.history: Louis XIV founded the hospital and church for wounded veterans in the late 17th century.
hotel_des_invalides.significance: French military history from armor to World Wars in adjoining galleries.
facts: The dome church requires a combined ticket with the museum.
Lawns toward the Seine are pleasant for a pause.

sainte_chapelle.description: Royal chapel famed for upper-level stained glass telling biblical narratives in jewel tones.
sainte_chapelle.history: Built by Louis IX in the 1240s to house Passion relics.
sainte_chapelle.significance: Gothic Rayonnant masterpiece—interior visits reward slow looking.
facts: Security queue is shared with the Conciergerie ticket combo.
Bright sun deepens the glass colors.

pont_neuf.address: Île de la Cité to Left and Right Banks
pont_neuf.description: Despite the name 'new bridge,' the oldest standing bridge across the Seine in Paris with mascarons along the parapets.
pont_neuf.history: Completed in the early 1600s under Henri IV.
pont_neuf.significance: Connects the tip of the island to both banks near the Louvre.
facts: Square du Vert-Galant sits on the downstream tip.
Night lighting reflects well for river photography.

palais_garnier.description: Second Empire opera house with marble staircases, Chagall-painted ceiling, and gilded auditorium.
palais_garnier.history: Opened in 1875; setting of Leroux's Phantom tale.
palais_garnier.significance: National ballet and opera venue plus self-guided daytime visits.
facts: Dress codes apply for evening performances.
The library-museum holds costume sketches.

champs_elysees.description: Grand avenue from Place de la Concorde to the Arc de Triomphe—luxury shops, cinemas, and parade route.
champs_elysees.history: Former market gardens became a ceremonial axis under successive regimes.
champs_elysees.significance: Bastille Day and finish line of the Tour de France.
facts: Sidewalks are long; metro stops break the walk.
Christmas lights draw heavy evening traffic.

grande_arche.description: Late 20th-century hollow cube monument closing the historic axis westward from the Arc de Triomphe.
grande_arche.history: François Mitterrand-era grands projets; opened in 1989.
grande_arche.significance: Business district landmark with rooftop perspectives on good weather days.
facts: Metro RER connections from central Paris are straightforward.
Hours for the roof vary—check ahead.

hotel_de_ville_paris.description: Neo-Renaissance city hall fronting a busy plaza once called Place de Grève.
hotel_de_ville_paris.history: Third Republic rebuilding after the 1871 fire during the Commune.
hotel_de_ville_paris.significance: Political heart of Paris with seasonal ice rink and exhibits in the courtyard.
facts: Free exhibitions often fill the Salle Saint-Jean.
Riots and celebrations both mark this square in French history.

conciergerie.description: Medieval royal palace turned revolutionary prison—Marie Antoinette's cell is commemorated here.
conciergerie.history: Kings ruled from this island complex before Versailles; Revolutionary Tribunal sat downstairs.
conciergerie.significance: Pairs with Sainte-Chapelle in the same Palais de la Cité compound.
facts: Audioguide explains trial procedures.
Gothic halls survive from the Capetian era.

louvre.description: World-famous museum in a former royal palace; glass pyramid entrance by I. M. Pei.
louvre.history: Fortress origins; Louvre palace grew across centuries; museum opened after the Revolution.
louvre.significance: From ancient near east to Renaissance painting—plan by wing or accept getting lost elegantly.
facts: Closed Tuesdays; prebook timed tickets.
Cour Napoléon is dramatic at dusk.

jardin_du_luxembourg.description: Formal French garden around the Senate palace—ponds, statues, and iconic green chairs.
jardin_du_luxembourg.history: Marie de' Medici commissioned the palace in the 1610s; public garden evolved over centuries.
jardin_du_luxembourg.significance: Neighborhood-scale calm between Latin Quarter and Saint-Germain.
facts: Model boats rent on the central fountain on fair days.
Some lawns are closed to sitting—signs explain zones.

place_vendome.description: Octagonal square with Vendôme column—center of high jewelry maisons and the Ritz.
place_vendome.history: Laid out under Louis XIV; column replaced after the Commune pulled down the original imperial statue.
place_vendome.significance: Architecture of continuous arcades defines luxury retail Paris.
facts: Peek into courtyards only where invited.
Napoleon's campaigns are cast in bas-relief up the column.

pantheon_paris.description: Neoclassical church turned secular temple housing the remains of distinguished French citizens.
pantheon_paris.history: Revolutionary redesign of Soufflot's church; Foucault's pendulum once hung beneath the dome.
pantheon_paris.significance: Panthéon burials read like a canon of literature, science, and resistance.
facts: Crypt visits are included in the main ticket.
The colonnaded porch echoes Rome's Pantheon.

pavillon_de_flore.description: Riverside pavilion at the Louvre's western end—part of the museum wing facing the Tuileries Garden.
pavillon_de_flore.history: Named for Flore; rebuilt after 19th-century fires; unified with the Louvre museum circuit today.
pavillon_de_flore.significance: Framing shot between garden allées and Seine traffic.
facts: Approach from the Tuileries for symmetry with the Carrousel arch.
Exhibits inside rotate with the Louvre program.

church_1_paris.description: A text-only church entry added for itinerary balance and slower walking routes.
facts: Text-only entry to keep PDF builds stable.
Can be replaced with a sourced church later.
church_1_paris.history: Added as a structured placeholder with substantive text (no image).
church_1_paris.significance: Helps diversify the guide with more sacred architecture stops.
